title: Define and enforce the identity boundary between ticket_id, tx_id, task_id, and session_id
plan_sections: Server-owned transaction variables vs client-managed inputs
goal: Align the runtime code with the 0.6.0 identity model so that ticket_id remains planning identity, tx_id remains exact server-issued runtime identity, task_id remains a helper compatibility label, and session_id remains secondary runtime context rather than a correctness anchor.
scope: Review how ticket_id, tx_id, task_id, and session_id are represented and propagated through the runtime implementation
Identify code paths that derive, reconstruct, reverse-map, or otherwise blur canonical transaction identity
Define the concrete enforcement rules needed for 0.6.0 implementation work
Update code paths that currently allow helper-style or planning identifiers to influence canonical runtime identity
Document any compatibility boundaries required for legacy data while preserving strict new-write semantics
out_of_scope: Implementing the full materialized-state schema redesign
Implementing the full checkpoint event redesign
expected_outputs: A precise mapping of where each identifier is introduced, stored, read, and used
A list of code paths that violate or risk violating the 0.6.0 identity boundary
Code changes that enforce exact tx_id continuity and remove identity ambiguity from new runtime behavior
A short implementation note describing any bounded historical compatibility behavior retained for legacy data
acceptance_criteria: New runtime behavior does not derive tx_id from ticket_id, task_id, session_id, or any other client-managed label
Continuation logic uses exact canonical tx_id rather than helper-style reverse mapping or heuristic reconstruction
task_id is treated only as a client-managed helper compatibility label and not as canonical transaction identity
session_id is not used as the primary basis for active transaction identity, active transaction selection, or deterministic resume correctness
ticket_id remains a planning/work-item identifier and is not promoted into canonical runtime identity
Any retained compatibility handling is explicitly bounded to historical data and does not weaken strict new-write semantics
notes: This ticket establishes the identity boundary that later runtime, resume, and compatibility tickets must preserve.
All implementation choices in this ticket must follow the plan's distinction between server-owned canonical protocol state and client-managed inputs.
If legacy compatibility requires normalization of historical identity formats, that normalization must be read-path-only and must not redefine the 0.6.0 canonical identity model.
